sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Dyspnoea refers to the sensation of shortness of breath or difficulty in breathing. It can be a subjective experience, meaning that it is based on the individual's perception of their breathing difficulty. Dyspnoea can range from mild to severe distress and can occur during rest or physical activity.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Distressed, anxious, fighting to breath, exhausted, altered level of consciousness
- Pale, sweaty and can be cyanosed
- Single words responses or unable to speak
- Stridor, crackles, wheeze, silent chest
- Breathing rate of < 6 or > 20
- Accessory muscle use, intercostal recession and/or tracheal tug, prolonged expiratory phases
- Tachycardia > 120 or bradycardia < 60

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Agitation, confusion, or oxygen-mask intolerance may reflect hypoxia, exhaustion, or evolving airway failure
- Oxygen should be titrated to clinical need rather than used or withheld according to a single rule
- Do not over-diagnose hyperventilation syndrome in the field when serious pathology such as pulmonary embolus, ACS, sepsis, pneumothorax, or toxic exposure may be present
- ACS can present primarily as dyspnoea, so a 12 lead ECG should be considered where appropriate
- Upper-airway oedema, neck trauma, or partial airway obstruction can progress rapidly to complete obstruction
- Limit interventions to those that preserve oxygenation, ventilation, and timely transport when the patient is critical
- Causes of dyspnoea to consider include airway obstruction, respiratory disease, cardiac failure, pulmonary embolus, metabolic acidosis, toxic exposure, neurological impairment, or thoracic trauma

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Continuous reassurance while minimising patient effort and unnecessary movement
- Treat life-threatening airway obstruction immediately
- If respiratory distress is caused by hazardous exposure, remove the patient or substance only if safe to do so and request appropriate hazard support early
- Position the patient to optimise breathing and airway protection
- Perform frequent vital signs, particularly respiratory rate, effort, and oxygen saturation
- Give oxygen only as clinically indicated and titrate to need
- Ventilate the patient if required
- Auscultate for diagnostic clues such as wheeze, crackles, stridor, pleuritic rub, or silent chest
- Apply ECG monitoring where available
- Consider Secondary Survey

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Manage the likely underlying condition if identified
- Follow the asthma, heart failure, chest trauma, anaphylaxis, ACS, or pulmonary embolus guideline as appropriate
- Consider advanced airway management if indicated and within scope
- Consider IV access if advanced therapy or deterioration is likely
- Obtain a 12 lead ECG when clinically appropriate
